title: Trigonometry: sine and cosine rules
summary: Right-angled trigonometry only takes you so far â€” most triangles that appear in exams and in the real world have no right angle at all. This lesson builds the full non-right-angled toolkit: a quick SOH-CAH-TOA recap, the sine rule and exactly when it applies, the ambiguous (SSA) case, the cosine rule and its rearrangement for an angle, the area formula $\frac{1}{2}ab\sin C$, and applications to bearings and angles of elevation and depression. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards marks, because in a calculation topic how you show your working decides how many marks you keep.
learningObjectives: Recall right-angled trigonometry (SOH-CAH-TOA) and Pythagoras, and recognise when a triangle is not right-angled.
Choose correctly between the sine rule and the cosine rule from the given information (AAS, ASA, SSA, SAS, SSS).
Apply the sine rule to find sides and angles, and detect and resolve the ambiguous (SSA) case.
Apply the cosine rule to find a side (SAS) and, rearranged, to find an angle (SSS), and use the area formula $\frac{1}{2}ab\sin C$.
Model problems involving bearings and angles of elevation and depression, and present working so method marks and follow-through protect the final answer.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Triangle Toolkit: Beyond 90 Degrees
simpleExplanation.summary: The sine and cosine rules are your instruments for solving ANY triangle, not just right-angled ones. Given a few measurements, they let you calculate every remaining side and angle. The whole skill is deciding which rule the given information calls for.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you are a surveyor mapping a triangular field. You cannot walk every edge and measure every corner, but you can measure a couple of lengths and an angle. The sine and cosine rules are the instruments that turn those few readings into the entire map â€” as long as you point the right instrument at the right measurements.
steps: Sketch the triangle. Label vertices with capitals ($A$, $B$, $C$) and each opposite side with the matching lowercase letter ($a$, $b$, $c$).
Read the given information. Do you have a side together with its opposite angle (a matching pair)? Then the sine rule works. If not, reach for the cosine rule.
Substitute the known values into the correct formula from the booklet, keeping angles in degrees unless told otherwise.
Solve, and if you used the sine rule to find an angle, check for the ambiguous case (a second, obtuse solution). Round only at the very end.

flashcards.back: $\sin\theta=\frac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}}$, $\cos\theta=\frac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}}$, $\tan\theta=\frac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}$. Valid ONLY in a right-angled triangle. For any other triangle use the sine or cosine rule.
$\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}$. Keep the unknown side on top. Needs at least one complete side-and-opposite-angle pair.
$\frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin B}{b}=\frac{\sin C}{c}$. Flip it so the unknown $\sin$ is on top. Then watch for the ambiguous case.

flashcards.front: When do I use the sine rule?
flashcards.back: When you have a matching pair â€” a side and its opposite angle â€” plus one more fact. This covers AAS and ASA, and the SSA (ambiguous) case.
$a^2=b^2+c^2-2bc\cos A$. Use it for two sides and the INCLUDED angle (SAS). The angle in the formula sits opposite the side you are finding.
$\cos A=\frac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2bc}$. Rearranged form. Use it when you know all three sides (SSS); $\arccos$ returns the correct angle even when it is obtuse.

flashcards.front: When do I use the cosine rule?
flashcards.back: When there is NO matching pair to start from: two sides and the included angle (SAS) to find the third side, or all three sides (SSS) to find any angle.
Area $=\frac{1}{2}ab\sin C$ â€” half the product of two sides and the sine of the angle BETWEEN them. The angle must be the included angle, or the formula is invalid.
Given two sides and a non-included angle, the sine rule can give two valid triangles. If $\sin B=k$ then $B_1=\arcsin k$ and $B_2=180^\circ-B_1$; keep $B_2$ whenever $A+B_2<180^\circ$.

In triangle $ABC$, side $a$ is opposite angle $A$, side $b$ opposite $B$, side $c$ opposite $C$ â€” each lies across the triangle from its partner. Mislabelling this is the most common source of lost marks.
Use the cosine rule. $\arcsin$ only returns acute angles, so the sine rule can miss an obtuse angle; $\arccos$ returns a unique value in $0^\circ$ to $180^\circ$ and cannot be fooled.

A bearing is measured clockwise from north as a three-figure angle (e.g. $070^\circ$, $250^\circ$). Sketch north lines at each point; alternate angles between parallel north lines usually unlock the triangle's interior angle.

The angle of elevation is measured UP from the horizontal to an object; the angle of depression is measured DOWN. They are equal (alternate angles), which lets you transfer an angle from observer to object.
Work in DEGREES unless the question states radians. A common silent error is leaving the calculator in radian mode, which makes every sine and cosine wrong.

quickCheck.prompt: You know all three side lengths of a triangle and want the largest angle. Which is the correct and safest tool?
options: The sine rule, finding the angle
The cosine rule, rearranged for the angle
SOH-CAH-TOA
Pythagoras' theorem
quickCheck.answer: The cosine rule, rearranged for the angle

quickCheck.prompt: In triangle $ABC$ you are given $a$, $b$ and the included angle $C$, and asked for side $c$. Which rule applies?
options: The sine rule
The cosine rule
Either â€” they always give the same work
Neither; you need another angle first

quickCheck.prompt: Using the sine rule you find $\sin B = 0.79$ from an SSA setup, giving $B \approx 52^\circ$. What must you also check?
options: Whether $B = 180^\circ - 52^\circ = 128^\circ$ also gives a valid triangle
Whether your calculator is in radians
Whether $b$ is the longest side
Nothing â€” the acute answer is always the only one
quickCheck.answer: Whether $B = 180^\circ - 52^\circ = 128^\circ$ also gives a valid triangle

quickCheck.prompt: To use Area $= \frac{1}{2}ab\sin C$ correctly, the angle $C$ must be:
options: Any angle in the triangle
The angle INCLUDED between sides $a$ and $b$
The largest angle
The angle opposite the longest side
quickCheck.answer: The angle INCLUDED between sides $a$ and $b$

quickCheck.prompt: You compute a side as $10.517\ldots$ m, then need it in a later part. What keeps you accurate?
options: Round to 10.5 m now and reuse 10.5
Carry the full unrounded value and round only the final answer
Round to 11 m to keep it simple
Use 10 m because it is a length
quickCheck.answer: Carry the full unrounded value and round only the final answer

faq.q: Can I use SOH-CAH-TOA or Pythagoras on any triangle?
faq.a: No. They are special cases that hold only for right-angled triangles. For any oblique (non-right-angled) triangle you must use the sine or cosine rule. If you spot a right angle, by all means use the quicker right-angled tools â€” but never force them onto a triangle that has no $90^\circ$ angle.

faq.q: How do I decide between the sine rule and the cosine rule?
faq.a: Look for a matching pair â€” a side and its opposite angle. If you have one (plus one more fact), the sine rule works: this is the AAS, ASA and SSA situations. If you have no pair to start from â€” two sides and the included angle (SAS), or all three sides (SSS) â€” use the cosine rule. A quick sketch with everything labelled makes the choice obvious.

faq.q: Are the formulae in the booklet, so do I still need to learn them?
faq.a: Both rules and the area formula are in the formula booklet, so you need not memorise them letter-perfect. What the booklet cannot do is choose the rule for you, substitute correctly, or spot the ambiguous case â€” that judgement is exactly what earns the method marks, and it is what this lesson trains.

faq.q: Why is the cosine rule better for finding an obtuse angle?
faq.a: The inverse sine on your calculator only returns angles between $-90^\circ$ and $90^\circ$, so it can never hand you an obtuse angle directly â€” you would have to remember to compute $180^\circ$ minus its answer. The inverse cosine returns a unique angle between $0^\circ$ and $180^\circ$, so the rearranged cosine rule gives the correct obtuse angle automatically, with no ambiguity.

faq.q: How do bearings turn into a triangle problem?
faq.a: Draw a north line at each point and mark the clockwise bearings. Because the north lines are parallel, alternate and co-interior angle facts convert the bearings into the interior angles of the triangle formed by the points. Once you have an interior angle plus some sides, it becomes an ordinary sine- or cosine-rule problem.

sections.content: You have already met right-angled triangles â€” Pythagoras' theorem and SOH-CAH-TOA. But most triangles in exams, in navigation, in surveying and in mechanics have no right angle at all. This lesson gives you the two rules that solve ANY triangle: the sine rule and the cosine rule, together with the area formula and the modelling contexts (bearings, elevation and depression) where they earn their keep. As in every calculation topic, the worked examples here are laid out exactly the way our engine marks your own practice: the method is on the page, mark by mark.
Recap: right-angled trigonometry

Before reaching for the new rules, always check whether the triangle already has a right angle â€” if it does, the faster tools still work. In a right-angled triangle, label the sides relative to the angle $\theta$ you care about: the hypotenuse (opposite the right angle), the opposite (across from $\theta$) and the adjacent (next to $\theta$). Then SOH-CAH-TOA applies, and Pythagoras relates the three sides.

$\sin\theta=\dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{hyp}}, \quad \cos\theta=\dfrac{\text{adj}}{\text{hyp}}, \quad \tan\theta=\dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}$
$\text{and} \quad \text{hyp}^2=\text{opp}^2+\text{adj}^2 \quad (\text{Pythagoras}).$

items: These three ratios and Pythagoras hold ONLY when the triangle has a $90^\circ$ angle.
To find an unknown angle, use the inverse function, e.g. $\theta=\arctan\left(\frac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}}\right)$.
If there is no right angle, do not force these â€” switch to the sine or cosine rule.

sections.content: The sine rule

The sine rule links each side to the sine of its opposite angle: across a whole triangle the ratio of a side to the sine of the angle facing it is constant. So a single complete pair â€” one side with its opposite angle â€” is the 'key' that unlocks the rest, provided you have one further piece of information.

For a triangle with angles $A$, $B$, $C$ and opposite sides $a$, $b$, $c$:
finding a side: $\dfrac{a}{\sin A}=\dfrac{b}{\sin B}=\dfrac{c}{\sin C}$
finding an angle: $\dfrac{\sin A}{a}=\dfrac{\sin B}{b}=\dfrac{\sin C}{c}$

items: Use the sine rule when you have two angles and any side (AAS or ASA).
You can also use it for two sides and a non-included angle (SSA) â€” but then check the ambiguous case.
Put the unknown on top: side-on-top form to find a side, sine-on-top form to find an angle.

sections.question: In triangle $PQR$, angle $P = 42^\circ$, angle $Q = 75^\circ$, and side $QR = p = 15$ cm. Find side $PR = q$, correct to 3 significant figures. [3]
sections.solution:
Sketch and label: we know the pair ($P$, $p$) and want side $q$, whose opposite angle $Q$ is given. That is a matching pair plus one more fact â€” the sine rule fits.

**Select the rule (side-on-top form):**
$\dfrac{q}{\sin Q}=\dfrac{p}{\sin P}$  [M1: sine rule chosen]

**Substitute:**
$\dfrac{q}{\sin 75^\circ}=\dfrac{15}{\sin 42^\circ}$  [A1: correct substitution]

**Solve:**
$q=\dfrac{15\sin 75^\circ}{\sin 42^\circ}=21.65\ldots$ cm

**Round at the end:** $q \approx 21.7$ cm.  [A1: answer]

**Final answer:** $q \approx 21.7$ cm.

sections.content: The ambiguous (SSA) case

The sine rule carries one famous trap. When you are given two sides and a non-included angle (SSA) and use the sine rule to find an angle, there may be TWO triangles that fit the data. This happens because $\sin\theta=\sin(180^\circ-\theta)$: your calculator's $\arcsin$ hands you the acute angle, but the obtuse partner can be equally valid. Always test it.

sections.question: In triangle $ABC$, angle $A = 32^\circ$, $a = 6$ cm and $b = 9$ cm. Find the possible sizes of angle $B$. [4]
sections.solution:
We have the pair ($A$, $a$) and a second side $b$ with no angle â€” this is SSA, so use the sine rule and be alert for two answers.

**Sine rule, angle-on-top form:**
$\dfrac{\sin B}{b}=\dfrac{\sin A}{a}$  [M1]

**Substitute and solve for $\sin B$:**
$\sin B=\dfrac{9\sin 32^\circ}{6}=0.7949\ldots$  [A1]

**First (acute) solution:**
$B_1=\arcsin(0.7949\ldots)=52.6^\circ$ (3 s.f.).  [A1]

**Test the obtuse partner:**
$B_2=180^\circ-52.6^\circ=127.4^\circ$. Check it fits: $A+B_2=32^\circ+127.4^\circ=159.4^\circ<180^\circ$, leaving a positive angle $C$, so the second triangle is valid.  [A1]

**Final answer:** $B \approx 52.6^\circ$ or $B \approx 127.4^\circ$ â€” both are genuine solutions.

sections.content: Whenever the sine rule gives you an angle, immediately write $180^\circ$ minus that angle and test it: if it still leaves room for the other known angle (their sum is under $180^\circ$), a second triangle exists and both answers score. The IB routinely reserves a mark for spotting the second case. Note the contrast with the cosine rule, which returns a single unambiguous angle.
The cosine rule

The cosine rule is Pythagoras' theorem with a correction term for triangles that are not right-angled â€” indeed if $A=90^\circ$ then $\cos A=0$ and it collapses back to $a^2=b^2+c^2$. Use it when there is no starting pair: a side sandwiched between two known sides (SAS), or all three sides known (SSS).

For a triangle with angles $A$, $B$, $C$ and opposite sides $a$, $b$, $c$:
finding a side: $a^2=b^2+c^2-2bc\cos A$
finding an angle (rearranged): $\cos A=\dfrac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2bc}$

items: Use the cosine rule for two sides and the included angle (SAS) to find the third side.
Use the rearranged form for three sides (SSS) to find any angle â€” it handles obtuse angles cleanly.
The angle $A$ in the formula must be opposite the side $a$ you are finding (or relating).

sections.question: A triangular plot has two sides of 7 m and 9 m meeting at an angle of $58^\circ$. (a) Find the length of the third side. (b) Hence find the smallest angle of the plot. [5]
sections.solution:
Let $b=7$, $c=9$ and the included angle $A=58^\circ$; call the unknown third side $a$. This is SAS, so use the cosine rule.

**(a) Third side.**
$a^2=b^2+c^2-2bc\cos A$  [M1: cosine rule chosen]
$a^2=7^2+9^2-2(7)(9)\cos 58^\circ$  [A1: correct substitution]
$a^2=49+81-126\cos 58^\circ=63.23\ldots$
$a=\sqrt{63.23\ldots}=7.95$ m (3 s.f.).  [A1: answer]

**(b) Smallest angle.** The smallest angle faces the shortest side, which is $7$ m â€” that is angle $B$, opposite $b=7$. With all three sides now known (SSS), use the rearranged cosine rule so there is no ambiguity. Carry the unrounded $a=7.9517\ldots$
$\cos B=\dfrac{a^2+c^2-b^2}{2ac}=\dfrac{63.23\ldots+81-49}{2(7.9517\ldots)(9)}$  [M1: rearranged cosine rule with substitution]
$\cos B=0.6651\ldots \;\Rightarrow\; B=\arccos(0.6651\ldots)=48.3^\circ$ (3 s.f.).  [A1: answer]

**Final answer:** third side $\approx 7.95$ m; smallest angle $\approx 48.3^\circ$.

sections.content: Area of a triangle, and applications

When you know two sides and the angle between them, you do not need the base-and-height picture at all: the area is half the product of the two sides and the sine of their included angle. The word 'included' is doing all the work â€” the angle must sit between the two sides you use, or the formula is wrong.
Area $=\dfrac{1}{2}ab\sin C$, where $C$ is the angle INCLUDED between sides $a$ and $b$.

These rules come alive in navigation and surveying. A bearing is an angle measured clockwise from north, always written with three figures (for example $070^\circ$ or $250^\circ$). By drawing a north line at each point and using alternate angles between the parallel north lines, bearings convert into the interior angles of a triangle. Similarly, the angle of elevation is measured up from the horizontal to an object, and the angle of depression down from the horizontal; the two are equal alternate angles, which lets you carry an angle from an observer to a distant point.

sections.question: A ship sails 12 km from port $A$ on a bearing of $050^\circ$ to point $B$, then 16 km on a bearing of $110^\circ$ to point $C$. Find the distance $AC$. [4]
sections.solution:
**Find the interior angle at $B$.** Draw north lines at $A$ and $B$. The bearing of $B$ from $A$ is $050^\circ$, so the back-bearing of $A$ from $B$ is $050^\circ+180^\circ=230^\circ$. The bearing of $C$ from $B$ is $110^\circ$. The interior angle $ABC$ is the turn between these directions at $B$:
$\angle ABC=230^\circ-110^\circ=120^\circ$.  [M1: correct interior angle]

**Now it is SAS:** sides $AB=12$ and $BC=16$ with the included angle $B=120^\circ$, and we want $AC=b$. Use the cosine rule.
$AC^2=12^2+16^2-2(12)(16)\cos 120^\circ$  [M1: cosine rule with substitution]
$AC^2=144+256-384(-0.5)=400+192=592$
$AC=\sqrt{592}=24.3$ km (3 s.f.).  [A1: value]

**Final answer:** $AC \approx 24.3$ km.  [A1: units and rounding]

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Choosing the wrong rule** â€” reaching for the sine rule on an SAS problem (two sides and the included angle) where there is no matching pair. No side-and-opposite-angle pair means the cosine rule.
**Missing the ambiguous case** â€” finding one acute angle from the sine rule in an SSA problem and stopping. Always test $180^\circ$ minus your angle; if it still fits, both answers score.
**Using a non-included angle in the area formula** â€” $\frac{1}{2}ab\sin C$ needs the angle BETWEEN sides $a$ and $b$. Any other angle gives the wrong area.

**Rounding too early** â€” quoting an intermediate side as, say, $7.95$ m and then feeding $7.95$ into the next part. Carry the full unrounded value and round only the final answer, or accuracy marks slip away.
**Mislabelling side and opposite angle** â€” putting an angle over the wrong side in the sine rule. Sketch the triangle and match each capital to its lowercase partner.
**Finding an obtuse angle with the sine rule** â€” $\arcsin$ only returns acute angles, so use the rearranged cosine rule for the largest angle to get the obtuse value directly.
**Radian mode** â€” leaving the calculator in radians silently corrupts every sine and cosine. Work in degrees unless the question says otherwise.

sections.content: Model answer â€” marked the way our engine marks it

On Paper 1 the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working â€” a method mark ($M$) for the correct approach, or an accuracy mark ($A$) for the right value, where an $A$ mark is dependent on the $M$ mark before it. Follow-through (FT) means that a right method carried out on a wrong earlier number still earns the marks that depend on it, and 'ISW' (ignore subsequent working) means a correct answer is not un-awarded if a student then writes something clumsy. But this protection only exists if the method is on the page. Study how each of the five marks below is earned by a specific line.

sections.question: In triangle $ABC$, $AB = 8$ cm, $AC = 11$ cm and angle $BAC = 40^\circ$. Find $BC$, then find the area of the triangle. [5]
sections.solution:
**Model answer â€” full working.**

Sides $AB$ and $AC$ meet at the given angle $BAC=40^\circ$, so $BC$ is the side opposite that included angle. Two sides and the included angle is SAS â€” use the cosine rule for $BC$, then the area formula.

**Find $BC$.**
$BC^2=AB^2+AC^2-2(AB)(AC)\cos(BAC)$
$BC^2=8^2+11^2-2(8)(11)\cos 40^\circ$
$BC^2=64+121-176\cos 40^\circ=50.18\ldots$
$BC=\sqrt{50.18\ldots}=7.08$ cm (3 s.f.).

**Find the area.** The angle $40^\circ$ is included between $AB$ and $AC$, so it feeds the area formula directly:
Area $=\tfrac{1}{2}(AB)(AC)\sin(BAC)=\tfrac{1}{2}(8)(11)\sin 40^\circ$
Area $=44\sin 40^\circ=28.28\ldots=28.3$ cm$^2$ (3 s.f.).

**Final answer:** $BC \approx 7.08$ cm; area $\approx 28.3$ cm$^2$.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 5 marks:**

- **M1 â€” cosine rule with substitution.** Awarded for writing $BC^2=8^2+11^2-2(8)(11)\cos 40^\circ$ â€” the right rule with the right numbers in the right places. This is a METHOD mark: it stands even if the arithmetic later slips.
- **A1 â€” value of $BC$.** Awarded for $BC \approx 7.08$ cm. The engine 'accepts answers that round correctly', so $7.08$ or $7.09$ cm score; it does not insist on a fixed number of decimals, only a correctly-rounded value.
- **M1 â€” area formula with substitution.** Awarded for $\tfrac{1}{2}(8)(11)\sin 40^\circ$, using the INCLUDED angle. Because the area here uses the original data ($8$, $11$, $40^\circ$) rather than $BC$, it is independent â€” a slip in $BC$ cannot infect it.
- **A1 â€” value of the area.** Awarded for $\approx 28.3$ cm$^2$. Equivalent exact forms such as $44\sin 40^\circ$ are accepted, and ISW means a correct value is not lost if the student then adds an unnecessary line.
- **FT protection.** Suppose a student states the cosine rule correctly but mis-keys the calculator and gets, say, $BC=7.24$ cm. They keep the first M1 (correct method). Any later step that correctly uses THEIR figure earns its mark on follow-through â€” a right method on a wrong number is not punished twice.

**Bottom line:** of the 5 marks, two are method marks that survive an arithmetic slip, the accuracy marks accept any correctly-rounded value, and follow-through shields a right-method / wrong-number answer. A student who writes only '$28.3$' with no working risks 3-4 marks if a number is off; a student who shows the cosine rule, the substitution, the area formula and its substitution keeps the method marks regardless.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way â€” analytically, mark by mark, with follow-through. So always show the method: name the rule, write the substitution, then the value with its unit. State the ambiguous-case check when you use the sine rule for an angle. The method marks and follow-through are what protect your score when the final number slips, and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 question marked: solve a full sine- or cosine-rule problem with working

subtopics.title: Use of the sine rule, $\frac{a}{\sin A} = \frac{b}{\sin B} = \frac{c}{\sin C}$
Use of the cosine rule, $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$; $\cos C = \frac{a^2 + b^2 - c^2}{2ab}$
subtopics.detail: Finding sides and angles in non-right-angled triangles.

subtopics.title: Area of a triangle as Area $= \frac{1}{2}ab \sin C$

questionBank.prompt: In triangle ABC, angle A = $30^\circ$, side $b = 8$ cm and side $a = 4\sqrt{2}$ cm. Find the two possible values for angle B.

markScheme.text: Attempt to use the sine rule: $\frac{\sin B}{8} = \frac{\sin 30^\circ}{4\sqrt{2}}$.
Finds the second possible value for B using $\sin B = \sin(180^\circ - B)$: $B = 180^\circ - 45^\circ = 135^\circ$.

questionBank.modelAnswer:
Using the sine rule, $ rac{\sin B}{b} =  rac{\sin A}{a}$.
Substituting the given values: $ rac{\sin B}{8} =  rac{\sin 30^\circ}{4\sqrt{2}}$.
We know that $\sin 30^\circ =  rac{1}{2}$.
So, $\sin B =  rac{8 \sin 30^\circ}{4\sqrt{2}} =  rac{8 	imes  rac{1}{2}}{4\sqrt{2}} =  rac{4}{4\sqrt{2}} =  rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.
The principal value for B is $\arcsin\left( rac{1}{\sqrt{2}}ight) = 45^\circ$.
Since $\sin 	heta = \sin(180^\circ - 	heta)$, the other possible value for B is $180^\circ - 45^\circ = 135^\circ$.
To confirm this is a valid triangle, we check that the sum of angles A and B is less than $180^\circ$. $30^\circ + 135^\circ = 165^\circ < 180^\circ$, so this is a valid solution.
The two possible values for angle B are $45^\circ$ and $135^\circ$.

questionBank.prompt: A triangular garden plot PQR has side PQ = 15 m, QR = 12 m and angle PQR = $110^\circ$.\n(a) Find the length of the side PR. [3]\n(b) Find the area of the garden plot. [3]

markScheme.text: (a) Attempt to use the cosine rule: $PR^2 = 15^2 + 12^2 - 2(15)(12)\cos(110^\circ)$.
(b) Attempt to use the area formula: Area = $\frac{1}{2}(15)(12)\sin(110^\circ)$.

questionBank.modelAnswer:
(a) Let the vertices be P, Q, R and the opposite sides be $r, p, q$. We have $r=15$, $p=12$ and angle $Q = 110^\circ$. We wish to find side $q$ (PR).
Using the cosine rule, $q^2 = p^2 + r^2 - 2pr \cos Q$.
$PR^2 = 12^2 + 15^2 - 2(12)(15)\cos(110^\circ)$.
$PR^2 = 144 + 225 - 360\cos(110^\circ)$.
Using a calculator, $PR^2 = 369 - 360(-0.34202...) = 369 + 123.127... = 492.127...$
$PR = \sqrt{492.127...} \approx 22.1839...$
The length of PR is $22.2$ m (to 3 significant figures).
(b) The area of the triangle is given by the formula Area = $ rac{1}{2}pr \sin Q$.
Area = $ rac{1}{2}(12)(15)\sin(110^\circ)$.
Area = $90 \sin(110^\circ)$.
Using a calculator, Area $\approx 90 	imes 0.93969... = 84.572...$
The area of the garden plot is $84.6$ m$^2$ (to 3 significant figures).

questionBank.prompt:
In triangle LMN, LM = $x$, MN = $x-2$ and LN = $x+2$. Angle LMN = $	heta$.
(a) Use the cosine rule to show that $\cos	heta =  rac{x-8}{2(x-2)}$. [3]
(b) Given that $\cos	heta =  rac{1}{4}$, find the value of $x$. [2]
(c) Hence, find the exact area of the triangle LMN. [2]

markScheme.text: (a) Correct substitution into cosine rule: $(x+2)^2 = x^2 + (x-2)^2 - 2x(x-2)\cos\theta$.
Expands and simplifies: $x^2+4x+4 = x^2 + x^2-4x+4 - 2x(x-2)\cos\theta$, leading to $8x - x^2 = -2x(x-2)\cos\theta$.

questionBank.modelAnswer:
(a) Using the cosine rule on triangle LMN, with angle LMN = $	heta$ opposite side LN:
$LN^2 = LM^2 + MN^2 - 2(LM)(MN)\cos	heta$
$(x+2)^2 = x^2 + (x-2)^2 - 2(x)(x-2)\cos	heta$
$x^2 + 4x + 4 = x^2 + (x^2 - 4x + 4) - 2x(x-2)\cos	heta$
$x^2 + 4x + 4 = 2x^2 - 4x + 4 - 2x(x-2)\cos	heta$
$4x + 4x = 2x^2 - x^2 + 4 - 4 - 2x(x-2)\cos	heta$
$8x = x^2 - 2x(x-2)\cos	heta$
$2x(x-2)\cos	heta = x^2 - 8x = x(x-8)$
Since $x$ is a side length, $x 
eq 0$. We can divide by $x$: $2(x-2)\cos	heta = x-8$.
Therefore, $\cos	heta =  rac{x-8}{2(x-2)}$.
(b) Given $\cos	heta =  rac{1}{4}$, we set the expressions equal:
$ rac{x-8}{2(x-2)} =  rac{1}{4}$
$4(x-8) = 2(x-2)$
$4x - 32 = 2x - 4$
$2x = 28$
$x = 14$.
(c) First, find $\sin	heta$. Using the identity $\sin^2	heta + \cos^2	heta = 1$: 
$\sin^2	heta = 1 - ( rac{1}{4})^2 = 1 -  rac{1}{16} =  rac{15}{16}$.
Since $	heta$ is an angle in a triangle, $\sin	heta$ must be positive, so $\sin	heta = \sqrt{ rac{15}{16}} =  rac{\sqrt{15}}{4}$.
The area of the triangle is given by Area = $ rac{1}{2}(LM)(MN)\sin	heta$.
With $x=14$, the sides are LM=14 and MN=12.
Area = $ rac{1}{2}(14)(12)\left( rac{\sqrt{15}}{4}ight) = 7 	imes 12 	imes  rac{\sqrt{15}}{4} = 84  rac{\sqrt{15}}{4} = 21\sqrt{15}$.

questionBank.prompt:
A vertical mast, PT, of height 10 m stands at point P on a horizontal triangular field ABC. The angle of elevation of the top of the mast, T, from vertex A is $20^\circ$. The angle of elevation of T from vertex B is $35^\circ$. The angle APB is $120^\circ$.
(a) Find the lengths of PA and PB. [2]
(b) Calculate the length of AB. [3]
(c) Find the area of the triangular field ABC, given that angle PBC is $40^\circ$ and angle PCB is $70^\circ$. [3]

markScheme.text: (a) Use of trigonometry in right-angled triangles TPA and TPB: $PA = \frac{10}{\tan(20^\circ)}$ and $PB = \frac{10}{\tan(35^\circ)}$.
(b) Attempt to use cosine rule in $\triangle$APB: $AB^2 = PA^2 + PB^2 - 2(PA)(PB)\cos(120^\circ)$.
(c) In $\triangle$PBC, finds $\angle BPC = 70^\circ$ and recognises it is isosceles, so $BC=PB=14.28...$ m.
Finds $\angle ABC$ by first finding $\angle ABP$ using sine rule in $\triangle APB$ and adding to $\angle PBC$. $\angle ABP = 40.31...^\circ$, so $\angle ABC = 80.31...^\circ$.
Correctly calculates area of $\triangle ABC = \frac{1}{2}(AB)(BC)\sin(\angle ABC) = 259$ m$^2$ (3 s.f.).

questionBank.modelAnswer:
(a) The mast PT is vertical, so $	riangle TPA$ and $	riangle TPB$ are right-angled at P.
In $	riangle TPA$, $	an(20^\circ) =  rac{PT}{PA} =  rac{10}{PA} \implies PA =  rac{10}{	an(20^\circ)} \approx 27.4747...$ m.
In $	riangle TPB$, $	an(35^\circ) =  rac{PT}{PB} =  rac{10}{PB} \implies PB =  rac{10}{	an(35^\circ)} \approx 14.2814...$ m.
To 3 s.f., $PA = 27.5$ m and $PB = 14.3$ m.
(b) In the horizontal triangle APB, we use the cosine rule to find the length of AB.
$AB^2 = PA^2 + PB^2 - 2(PA)(PB)\cos(120^\circ)$.
$AB^2 = (27.4747...)^2 + (14.2814...)^2 - 2(27.4747...)(14.2814...)\cos(120^\circ)$.
$AB^2 = 754.86... + 203.96... - 784.86...(-0.5) = 958.82... + 392.43... = 1351.25...$
$AB = \sqrt{1351.25...} \approx 36.759...$ m.
To 3 s.f., $AB = 36.8$ m.
(c) In $	riangle PBC$, the sum of angles is $180^\circ$. So, $\angle BPC = 180^\circ - \angle PBC - \angle PCB = 180^\circ - 40^\circ - 70^\circ = 70^\circ$.
Since $\angle BPC = \angle PCB = 70^\circ$, $	riangle PBC$ is an isosceles triangle with $BC = PB = 14.2814...$ m.
To find the area of $	riangle ABC$, we need the angle $\angle ABC = \angle ABP + \angle CBP$. We know $\angle CBP = 40^\circ$.
In $	riangle APB$, we use the sine rule to find $\angle ABP$:
$ rac{\sin(\angle ABP)}{PA} =  rac{\sin(\angle APB)}{AB}$
$\sin(\angle ABP) =  rac{PA \sin(120^\circ)}{AB} =  rac{27.4747... 	imes \sin(120^\circ)}{36.759...} \approx 0.6470...$
$\angle ABP = \arcsin(0.6470...) \approx 40.316...^\circ$.
So, $\angle ABC = 40.316...^\circ + 40^\circ = 80.316...^\circ$.
Area of $	riangle ABC =  rac{1}{2}(AB)(BC)\sin(\angle ABC)$.
Area $=  rac{1}{2}(36.759...)(14.2814...)\sin(80.316...^\circ) \approx 258.78...$ m$^2$.
To 3 s.f., the area of the field ABC is 259 m$^2$.
